properties.remark: First 58 mph or greater gust at 1053 AM. Peak gust at 1111 AM. Last gust at 1210 PM.
First 58 mph or greater gust at 1115 AM. Peak gust at 212 PM. Final gust at 216 PM.
First 58 mph or greater gust at 11:30 AM. Peak gust of 64 mph at 12:48 PM. Last gust so far at 2:51 PM.
Mesonet station 35 Clarendon 2W.
Mesonet station 121 Lake McClellan 2NW.
This tornado was photographed and filmed by multiple sources. It remained in a field and resulted in no known damage.

The tornado developed in a field to the south of County Road M. It then crossed County Road M, where it snapped three power poles and overturned a portion of an irrigation pivot. The tornado then crossed Highway 83 where it caused loss of roofing material to a roundtop quonset. As the tornado continued to track to the northeast, it threw mud on several power poles and deposited debris along County Road L. The tornado tracked northeast before dissipating in a field. Video footage exists that confirms this tornado was rather wide and possibly had multiple vortices. The tornado was rated an EF1 with estimated peak winds of 105mph, a path length of 1.59 miles and width of 100 yards.

Videos/pictures confirm a tornado touched down south of Perryton. There is damage to a barn, a center pivot was knocked over, and the roof on the hospital on the south side of Perryton was damaged. A survey team is planned to further investigate.
Quarter size hail fell along Indiana Street.
Corrects previous hail report from 1 S Perryton. Reported came from over the phone.
Report of quarter size hail near the Southwest Loop Road and Spur 192.

This brief tornado touched down shortly after the first tornado as the mesocyclone occluded. Damage to a barn included loss of a door and some lifted roof panels near County Rd K, with additional damage to the Ochiltree Hospital where shingles and some roof material were blown off just before the tornado lifted. This tornado was rated an EF-0 with estimated peak winds of 80mph and path length of 0.72 miles. Estimated path width was 30 yards.

This quick, brief tornado touched down to the north of I-40. Spotters had reported a funnel cloud had crossed I-40, but no damage was noted. The tornado caused damage to the Dalton O Gorman Rodeo Arena. Here it caused roof damage where a large section of the eastern half of the roof was blown off and the south facing wall was blown down. No other damage occurred in this area so it is believed to be a narrow path. The tornado lifted soon thereafter. This tornado was rated an EF-1 with estimated winds of 100 mph. The path length was 0.18 miles and path width of 25 yards.
Reported came from over the phone.

This tornado touched down briefly in ranch land to the west southwest of Clarendon. The damage appeared to be mostly from damaged mesquite brush and perhaps some fencing material. Survey information was obtained from local officials as access was hard due to private land and terrain. The tornado was also documented on chaser/spotter footage and appeared to not be on the ground but for a couple of minutes before lifting.
A very brief tornado occurred at this location and was confirmed by video.
Time estimated off of radar.

This tornado touched down after the initial parent tornado occluded. Based on spotter/chaser video, it appears to have developed in about the same locations as the first Clarendon tornado. It became wide, likely going over the same locations as the previous tornado. It then started to follow the rim of the mesocyclone, likely being more of a satellite tornado around the main mesocyclone. The tornado was recorded as being on the ground for about 15 minutes before finally lifting. It likely did not move very fast before finally becoming corkscrew in nature before lifting. Damage seems to be limited to mesquite brush damage. However, the worst known damage was to a water pumping windmill which was toppled and destroyed by the tornado.
Tornado at this location began around 4:10 PM and lifted around 4:19 PM, confirmed by video.

This brief tornado began around 5:14 PM, had a path length of 1.01 miles and a path width of 25 yards. The tornado lifted around 5:16 PM about 13 miles south southeast of Spearman. This tornado did not produce any damage and was rated EF-Unknown. The tornado was documented by law enforcement reports and storm chaser video footage.
This brief tornado began around 5:14 PM and lifted around 5:16 PM, confirmed by video.
Social Media Report.
Slightly larger than quarter size hail fell at the Lowes Market along Highway 207 in Spearman.
Received images of a large tree downed onto a brick fence which also suffered damage from the collision.

This tornado touched down to the west of County Road 24 where it snapped the top branches and trunk of a couple of hardwood trees. It then traveled south, crossing County Road 24 and lifting the roof off of a building at the Storage Depot. It continued south and caused minor damage to a nearby shed. The tornado lifted before it reached Highway 207. Max estimated winds were 85mph. Path length was 0.34 miles and width was 30 yards.

This tornado began at 6:46 PM. The path length was 0.22 mile and the path width was around 15 yards. The tornado moved east across the Spearman High School football field and into open country. The tornado damaged one small shed and pushed a utility trailer onto its side but did not damage power lines along the road. The tornado lifted around 6:48 PM approximately 1 mile south southeast of Spearman. The estimated peak wind speed was 80 mph, which yields a rating of EF0 for this tornado. This tornado was also confirmed by multiple videos.

This tornado occurred on the northeast side of the Spearman. It initially touched down near Brandt Street and Kenneth Avenue(FM 759 760). Here it snapped some tree branches, lifted some carports and caused minor fence damage. It then traveled north along County Road 25, where it snapped a few power poles and this is where it was given its highest rating. As it neared Highway 15, it collapsed an empty grain silo and caused some minor shingle damage to a few roofs along County Road 25. The tornado turned east, crossing Highway 15 and caused additional minor roof damage and snapped branches to hardwood trees. It finally lifted just to the east of Highway 15. Max estimated winds were 98 mph. Rating was EF1. Estimated length was 1.51 miles, width was 75 yards.
This very brief tornado began at 6:55 PM and lasted around 15 to 20 seconds, confirmed by video.
Report from social media.

Multiple photos submitted to local media of various tree damage. Three inch diameter tree limb snapped. Couple of dead or diseased trees uprooted. One of witch fell on and damaged a residents chain link fence.

House was hit with dime to quarter size hail, but the worst part of the storm stayed just west of their location.
Wind gust associated with downburst from anvil ahead of the storm. Peak wind gust lasted for several minutes.
Hail broke out windows of a well work truck.
Corrects previous hail report from 10 S Texline. Timed based on radar.

This gust was from decaying thunderstorms, not so much a strong storm with a strong updraft. This gust is assumed to be from rain falling through a dry low level layer creating cooling and strong sinking air.

A storm chaser photographed a large cone tornado that was in open country grasslands and no damage was reported or likely occurred given the location (EFu). The tornado was on the ground for over a minute before becoming rain wrapped obscuring it from view. Location is estimated based on radar and reports.
Report from mPING: Quarter (1.00 in.).
Actual measured size was 1.15 inches.

Corrects previous tornado report from 4 WNW Sanford. NWS employee reported tornado on the ground. This tornado formed over Lake Meredith. The track of the tornado was parallel to the shoreline, before moving north-northeast into an inlet called Bugbee. The tornado then lifted as it approached the shoreline just south of Bugbee Cove. Numerous pictures confirmed tornado, and also produce some tree limb damage near the shoreline of Bugbee Cove.
NWS employee reported tornado on the ground.

A second EF0 tornado formed and moved northeast between Lake Meredith and the Bugbee community. Was on the ground for about 2 minutes with a path width of 75 yards and path track of 0.8 mile. A few mesquite tree limbs were snapped and other floral damage was indicated.
Quarter size hail fell in Booker.
Report from mPING: Quarter (1.00 in.).
Report from mPING: Quarter (1.00 in.).
Golf ball size hail approximately 7 miles south of Pampa on Texas Highway 70.
Accumulating hail of quarter size.

Two large trees down near the intersection of 34th and Georgia Street.

Picture sent to us of 8-12 inch tree branches snapped and blocking road.
Vehicle stranded with water flowing under bridge on East Amarillo Blvd. Reported on Veoci.
Power lines down on South Washington Street near the 3400 block. Time estimated by radar.
The mesonet showed gusts of 58-60mph several times from 5:37pm through 5:50pm.
Photos via Facebook of downed electric line resulting in additional damage to paneling on a house. Small trees were also blown over.
Water rescues ongoing due to flash flooding. Pictures provided on social media show water over roadways with stalled cars.
Sunray mesonet measured 58 mph gust with lightning in the area.
Video on social media shows a grain silo destroyed and laying across the south bound lane of highway 87.

Emergency manager relayed report via Slack. Dispatchers report large near baseball size hail on Highway 54 between Goodwell and Texhoma. Said it stopped before Goodwell. Said enough to cover roadway and she had major cracks in windshield.
Local EM reports areas of flooding in and around Beaver, OK. Multiple lane and road closures ongoing in the area due to flooding, especially HWY 23. Bar ditches overflowing onto roadways.

Corrects previous flash flood report from 2 NNW Guymon. Picture sent through Facebook. Road completely covered by water. This water extend fully into the N side of town with widespread flooding still present at 4:30pm.

All homes in Goodwell are flooded.

The roof and part of the wall of an old shed were blown west onto the neighboring property.

Hail started around 615 pm and lasted several minutes at the Pantex Plant. Small dents were observed on vehicles in the parking lot after the storm passed.
Pantex 4WSW mesonet measured peak gust of 59 mph with first severe gust observed at 634 PM.

Picture received from local law enforcement of approximately half dollar sized hail. Also reported accumulating hail up to one foot deep near FM 2161 and county road 7 in Carson county. Time estimated based on radar.

Hailed for about 15 minutes at an outdoor adventure park on the north rim of the Canyon with up to around ping pong size hail reported. At least 3 RVs were damaged and had skylights blown out. Porta Potties were blown about 40 feet.
Several large branches were broken off of hardwood tree and at least one tree was uprooted and another large hardwood tree was snapped near the base of the trunk along E0070 Rd.
Corrects previous tstm wnd gst report from 11 WSW Bootleg. First severe wind gust observed at the Mesonet.
First severe wind gust observed at the Mesonet.
Corrects previous tstm wnd gst report from 11 WSW Bootleg. Peak weak gust observed at the mesonet so far.
Peak weak gust observed at the mesonet so far.
Strongest wind gust occurred at 817 PM. Multiple gusts of 60 mph or greater occurred for nearly 10 minutes.
Severe wind gusts occurred for 3 minutes with the strongest gust at 821 PM.
Wind gusts of 58 mph or greater occurred for 3 minutes with the peak being at 909 PM.
Grain silo roof was ripped off and the west side wall of the silo was caved in. Time estimated based on radar.
Reports of interstate 40 underneath the Bell Street bridge being flooded with cars stranded.
I-40 at Bell street is flooded out and APD has that portion of the highway closed in both directions; water rescues are ongoing at multiple underpasses.
Report relayed to our office by the Cimarron County Emergency Manager. Time was estimated based on radar data.
Received a photo on social media of an older telephone pole snaped at its base.

Four power poles were snapped by thunderstorms winds along FM 51 near the Adobe Walls Gin all laying towards the road. Based on this damage winds are estimated between 70 and 80 mph most likely from a localized microburst.
About five power poles were snapped by thunderstorms winds along FM 1541.

Widespread wind damage has been reported across the city of Perryton. Damage consist of uprooted or snapped trees many of them healthy trees. Several aluminum car covers were damaged and moved with some minor damage to vehicles that were parked under them. Some buildings had aluminum siding ripped off. It is unknown how many power poles have been damaged at this time but there was one instance of a tree laying on powerlines. This damage is estimated to have occurred between 1850CDT and 1930CDT with several different gusts occurring. Based on damages wind speeds are estimated to be between 70 and 80 mph.
This was the first wind gust exceeding 58 mph up to 66 mph. There were subsequent gusts up to 71 mph through 1920CDT.
A photo of a landspout was submitted via social media. No damage was reported.
Storm chaser submitted multiple videos of a landspout. No damage was reported.
Weak echo return with a possible dry microburst. Not a strong thunderstorm, but a unique environmental interaction from a FEW090 cloud deck.
Wind gusts of 58 mph or greater were measured for 5 minutes beginning at 627 PM.
Multiple 2 to 3 inch tree branches broken off in Stratford.
Picture sent through social media of accumulating hail along I-27 between Canyon and Happy. Biggest hail stones measured were ping pong ball size.
62 mph wind gust observed from a decaying thunderstorm at the Dozier mesonet.
Public posted picture of hail measured at 1.5 inches. Timing and location are estimates pending further details from reporter.
Public reported 1 to 1.25 inch hail in Pampa with ponding of water on streets.

Law enforcement reported Marshall Drive (County Road P) is closed due to part of the road has been washed out one mile east of where the pavement section of the road ends. Road P was washed out at Elk Creek. The flash flood was likely 200 feet wide where the road crosses Elk Creek.
